arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-addr2line readelf 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'binutils|llvm' | head -5

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
# Try Microsoft symbol server with ELF BuildId format
BUILDID="4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801"
# Try downloading the symbol file
curl -sSL -o /tmp/libcoreclr_sym.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" -w "\nHTTP_CODE:%{http_code}\n" 2>&1 | tail -5
arguments.description: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
The tombstone shows a crash in a .NET Android app (CoreCLR-based). The crash is a SIGSEGV at address 0x48 (null pointer dereference with offset). The backtrace has frames in `libcoreclr.so` with BuildId `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`.

To symbolicate these frames, I need the debug symbols for `libcoreclr.so`. The approach would be:

1. Use the BuildId to find the matching symbols. The BuildId is `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`.


2. Microsoft publishes .NET runtime symbol packages, so I can try downloading symbols from their symbol server. The symbols might be available through NuGet packages or Microsoft's symbol server at `https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/`, though the exact URL format for ELF files may differ from the standard Windows format.

I have the BuildId `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`, and I can use this to construct the proper symbol server URL or try using debuginfod, which is the standard approach for fetching debug info from ELF binaries. The real goal here is to map those PC offsets to actual function names so I can symbolicate the stack frames.

arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line llvm-addr2line readelf 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; apt list --installed 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'binutils|llvm' | head -5
